speaker: interviewer
text: March 27th, 1995, survivor Lewis Fagen, tape number five. You were telling me about your son and his schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arthur graduated from Curtis and Wesleyan University simultaneously. He was 21 years old. And Christoph von Dohnanyi, who was the head of the Frankfurt Opera, was in New York. And he was inquiring about hiring an assistant, and Arthur was recommended, and he hired him. And after the summer of 1951-- no-- excuse me, 1972, Arthur moved to Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, on our business trips, which were twice a year-- we were taking twice a year to Europe-- we also visited Arthur in Frankfurt. And at the same time, we visited with Schindler. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We used to stay in Frankfurt four, five days. And practically every day we have-- we met-- we were meeting with Schindler. By the way, 1957, Schindler came to New York to graduation of Alex Rosner, who was a little boy in camp. And he was graduating from college, and he was-- and Schindler was invited to the graduation. And we threw a party for him and in our garden and invited several people. And we also, at that time, were planning to establish foundation for him, so he would have some kind of an income. But eventually, nothing came of it. But we used to meet Schindler twice a year in Frankfurt, whenever we visited Arthur. In the meantime, Lester went to Amherst College, from which he graduated. And he went to Jewish Theological Seminary on a Amherst College scholarship. He graduated from Jewish Theological Seminary. At that point, he decided he does not want to be a rabbi. And he went to Columbia Law School, from which he graduated in top 10%. In the meantime, he got married to Trudy, whom he knew since high school. And they decided to move-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- while Arthur was at Columbia Law School, he was already married to Trudy, they had a little, little daughter, Shoshana, who was born in the Bronx, while they lived in the Bronx. And then they-- after graduation, they decided to move to Boston. They like Boston. And-- now I don't blame them that they like it. Because it's a small city that has everything to offer that a large city has to offer. And Lester got a job in a law firm, and then another law firm. And he eventually joined a third law firm, in which he became a partner. Very active in, in Jewish life. They have a-- their own klezmer band, where they play at weddings and bar mitzvahs. And all the money goes to charities. And they really lead a wonderful life. Trudy is actually a psychologist and music therapist. And she has her own private practice. She shows teachers music therapy. They lead a very nice life in Boston. Arthur married in-- while he was in Holland, music director in, of Enschede Opera, married Paulette, whom he met in Italy-- Jewish girl, Paulette Berman from Chicago-- who was a singer, opera singer. And they married. And she gave birth to Alicia, 17 years ago, which was 1978, in Holland. And this was the cutest girl you could ever meet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And eventually, Arthur became music director of Queens Symphony, New York. And he was also music director of Antwerp Opera and Ghent in Belgium. They lived in Belgium for a while, where Rebecca was born. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually, they moved back to the States, where Arthur became musical director of Queens Symphony. In the meantime, he was traveling all over the world. He conducted everywhere from Beijing to, to, to Lisbon to, to Munich to-- all, all over the place. Right now he's in Oslo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lester also traveled many times to Israel, and has a very, very, very good practice in, in, in Boston. After all those years, my father passed away in 1993, I was in business with him. And in 1991, after I bought the place in Florida-- and I had it for three years-- I decided that I like it so much here that's I'm going to sell my house, and phase out of my business. And decision was made. We sold the house in Hewlett Harbor. It was a big house with five bedrooms. And I was phasing out of my business for a couple of years. And I am practically retired. Not 100%, but 95% already. And to-- we establish our life here. We've spend five months in New York and seven months in Florida. We are residents of Florida. We lead a very nice life here, we love every minute of it. We participate in sports-- golf, tennis, what have you. We take courses at the University. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have subscriptions at the Philharmonic. People don't believe in New York that Florida has a lot to offer, which it has. And we have a wonderful, wonderful retirement here. And we visit-- we are spend five months with our children in Hewlett-- they live in Long Island-- with Paulette and Arthur and their three daughters. Now they have three daughters-- Alicia, Rebecca, and Gabriela, who is only six months old. And Lester has three daughters-- Shoshana, Ari, and Lila, who is two years old. And we visit them in Boston, they visit us. They come to, to, to Florida. Arthur with his family are coming for Passover. Lester we have seen in January, we were there in Boston. And it's working out very well. And we have a lot of naches-- our children, our sons, daughter-in-laws, grandchildren, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message you'd like to leave for future generations about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I wanted to, to say that they should be proud of the heritage. That they should keep alive the history of the Holocaust. They should teach-- my grandchildren should teach their grand-- their children and grandchildren about the history of Holocaust. Because there's so much revisionism in the world today that somebody's trying to disprove of it. Once we are dead, and we are not alive anymore-- and we'll--this will be shortly-- the world will not believe of the horrors that happened to people during the Holocaust. So they have to keep that alive. And I know that my, my, my children and my grandchildren will do it. I know it. I have enough confidence in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for taking the time to tell us your fascinating story. We appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is my wife Rena, whom I met over 55 years ago. We went to the same school. We were friends since then. We lived on the same block. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in camps together. We were at Schindler's camp together. At the age of 16, I decided that I'm going to marry her. And I certainly did, after I met her again in 1947-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --in New York. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- we had many, many good years together. We raised wonderful children. We have great grandchildren. We have great daughters-in-law. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we certainly had a very, very, very nice life. We traveled a lot together. We did everything together. We went everywhere together. Even on business, my wife went always with me. And in the back of me, you can see the paintings that she painted. She's  a terrific painter, very capable girl. She can do anything, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is even a pretty good tennis player. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's about it. Now can you say something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that all through our married life, this-- this is-- I have a most wonderful, most caring husband. And again, we, we brought up wonderful two sons, of whom we are extremely proud-- of them, their families, our daughter-in-laws, our, our granddaughters. Just we have a lot to be thankful for. It was worthwhile to, to survive the war, to live to that point and to have that much joy from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I, I didn't mention that my mother passed away last year in January, at the age of 95. I think she was, at the time, the oldest Schindler survivor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was January of 1994. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's about it. This book, 'Schindler's Legacy,' is a story of 32 survivors of Schindler's, from Schindler's list. And among them, is a story on Rena and myself, a whole chapter. By the way, we were-- besides being on 'Schindler's List,' we were invited also by Steven Spielberg to Jerusalem, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the stone placing on Schindler's grave in Jerusalem, which was in 1993, in May.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that book, there are a couple of pictures. And one picture is of myself and my sister, when I was six, seven years old. And another picture is our marriage pictures, Rena's and my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself and my sister, when I was probably no more than six or seven years old. There is another picture of Rena and myself, when we were married. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] No, here is a picture of-- here is a picture of Schindler, entertained in our garden in Woodmere in 1957. You can see at the picture I am kneeling, and Rena is standing right above me. Schindler is next to Rena. Then Rena's cousin and Herman Rosner, the musician, the violinist, who wasn't shown in 'Schindler's List,' is on the right. And Rena's mother is next to Schindler. And next to her is Mrs. Gerner, Rena's aunt. And behind her, George Scheck, husband of her cousin. This was 1957, in Woodmere. And then there is a picture, our picture-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah, this is our wedding picture, in June of 1949. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can see how young I was at the time. This picture was taken in our garden in 1957, when Schindler was visiting in the United States. And we lived, at that time, in Woodmere, Long Island. On the right is standing Herman Rosner-- Henry Rosner, the violinist in 'Schindler's List.' Next to him is Pola Scheck, Rena's cousin. I am kneeling below. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next to Pola is Rena, my wife. Next to Rena is standing Schindler, Oskar Schindler. Next to him, my mother in-law Bella Schonthal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Behind her, you can see a head of David Fishman. And next is Adela Gerner, my wife's aunt. And next to her, George Scheck, Pola's husband. This is a picture of my parents. My father, on the right, Jack, who was called [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland, who passed away October 3, 1983. And then my mother Natalie, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who passed away January 2, 1994, at the age of 95. My father expired when he was 87 years old. And they both survivors of Schindler's. These are my two sons-- Arthur the conductor, opera and symphony conductor on the right. Lester, the great attorney on the left. I am very proud of both of them. This is a picture of my sons, their spouses, and my six granddaughters. On the right is Paulette, Arthur's wife. Next to Paulette is standing Rebecca. Right above Rebecca is my son Arthur. Right below him is my granddaughter Ari, who is the daughter of Lester, holding Gabriela, who is the daughter Arthur and Paulette. Right above them is Alicia, who is 17, Arthur's the oldest daughter. Next to her is Shani, Shoshana, Lester's daughter. And above her is Lester. And next to Lester is Trudy, his wife. And below is Lila, Lester's daughter. This is a picture of my six granddaughters. On the right, you can see Ari holding Lila. Above them, Alicia. Next to Alicia, Shoshana, holding Gabriela. And on the left, Rebecca. This is a picture taken in 1994 in Miami Beach, when Mrs. Emilie Schindler was honored. Rena is sitting next to Mrs. Schindler, and I am standing above. This is picture of Rena and myself, taken in- in our garden in Boca Raton, in 1993.
